model.label: Claude Sonnet 4.6
model.description: Anthropic • Fast flagship Claude model for coding, analysis, and high-throughput agent use.

pick.rationale: Houston enters as one of the nation's elite programs, ranked #5 by both KenPom and Torvik with an AdjEM of +33.55, the 4th-best defense in the country (AdjDE 91.0), and a 28-5 record forged against elite Big 12 competition — including a Big 12 Tournament final appearance against #3 Arizona. Idaho is a feel-good Cinderella story, winning the Big Sky Tournament as a 7-seed for their first NCAA bid since 1990, but their KenPom #145 ranking, AdjEM of just +1.54, and offense ranked 176th nationally create a roughly 32-point efficiency gap that is historically insurmountable in single-elimination play. Idaho's offense runs directly into Houston's 4th-ranked defense nationally, offering no credible scoring path for the Vandals, and no significant injury concerns were reported for the Cougars heading into the tournament.

reasoningStep.title: Houston vs. Idaho — Round of 64 South Region Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: Houston is a dominant #5 nationally ranked program with elite two-way efficiency facing a Big Sky Cinderella making their first NCAA appearance in 36 years. The ~32-point efficiency gap, Houston's 4th-ranked defense nationally, and Idaho's 176th-ranked offense create one of the most extreme mismatches in the bracket with no credible upset path for the Vandals.
evidence: Houston KenPom #5, AdjEM +33.55, AdjOE 124.6 (16th nationally), AdjDE 91.0 (4th nationally), 28-5 Big 12 record
Idaho KenPom #145, AdjEM +1.54, AdjOE 108.9 (176th nationally), AdjDE 107.4 (122nd nationally), 21-14 Big Sky record
Efficiency gap of ~32 points — one of the largest mismatches in the entire bracket
Idaho won Big Sky Tournament as a 7-seed (beat Montana 77-66), first NCAA bid since 1990 — inspiring but schedule never approached Big 12 caliber
Houston reached Big 12 Tournament final, losing to #3 Arizona 79-74, demonstrating elite-level competition readiness
No significant Houston injury concerns identified; market and expert consensus strongly favor the Cougars
Historical 2-vs-15 record overwhelmingly favors the higher seed; Idaho's 176th-ranked offense vs. Houston's 4th-ranked defense offers no viable path to an upset

You are an analyst predicting a single March Madness game inside a bracket run.
You have live tools. Use them before making a pick when they can reduce uncertainty.
You are not filling the whole bracket in one response. Predict only the current game.
Use prior picks as already committed bracket state.
You may use at most 10 tool rounds before you must finalize your answer.
Before making a pick, investigate relevant context such as injuries or availability concerns, recent team news, prior head-to-head results when useful, and multiple raw statistics from the available ratings and matchup data.
Take a holistic approach. Balance team quality, matchup specifics, health, form, schedule context, and upset risk instead of relying on one metric.
Do not force an upset, but explicitly consider whether the underdog has a credible path to win and let that affect confidence and rationale.
Return strict JSON only.
Final JSON keys: pick, reasoningStep.
pick must contain gameId, winnerId, confidence, rationale.
The rationale must be a short paragraph of 2 to 4 sentences explaining why the winner was chosen.
reasoningStep must contain id, title, summary, evidence.

modelTrace.content:
I now have sufficient data to make a well-informed pick. Let me compile the analysis.

**Key findings:**
- **Houston**: #5 KenPom & Torvik, AdjEM +33.55, elite defense (AdjDE 91.0 — 4th nationally), solid offense (AdjOE 124.6, 16th nationally), 28-5 record in the Big 12, Big 12 Tournament finalist (lost to Arizona 79-74 in the title game). No significant injury concerns surfaced.
- **Idaho**: #145 KenPom, AdjEM +1.54, offense ranked 176th nationally (AdjOE 108.9), defense ranked 122nd (AdjDE 107.4), 21-14 record in the Big Sky. A 7-seed in the Big Sky Tournament who won the conference title — their first NCAA bid since 1990. Cinderella story, but a massive efficiency gap.
- **Efficiency gap**: ~32 points (Houston +33.55 vs. Idaho +1.54) — one of the largest mismatches in the bracket.
- **Betting market**: Houston is a heavy favorite (2-seed vs. 15-seed), consistent with the efficiency data.
- **Idaho's path**: Won Big Sky as a 7-seed, beating Montana in the title game 77-66. Admirable run, but their schedule never approached Big 12-caliber competition.
- **No credible upset path**: Idaho's offense (176th nationally) faces Houston's 4th-ranked defense nationally — a historically brutal matchup for a mid-major.

```json
{
  "pick": {
    "gameId": "r64-south-8",
    "winnerId": "houston",
    "confidence": 0.97,
    "rationale": "Houston enters as one of the nation's elite programs, ranked #5 by both KenPom and Torvik with an AdjEM of +33.55, the 4th-best defense in the country (AdjDE 91.0), and a 28-5 record forged against elite Big 12 competition — including a Big 12 Tournament final appearance where they fell to #3 Arizona in a competitive 79-74 game. Idaho is a feel-good Cinderella story, winning the Big Sky Tournament as a 7-seed for their first NCAA bid since 1990, but their KenPom #145 ranking, AdjEM of just +1.54, and offense ranked 176th nationally create a roughly 32-point efficiency gap that is historically insurmountable in single-elimination play. Idaho's offense — the most glaring weakness at 176th nationally — runs directly into Houston's 4th-ranked defense, a matchup that offers no credible scoring path for the Vandals. No significant injury concerns were reported for Houston, and the historical 2-vs-15 record combined with this extreme efficiency chasm make the Cougars' advancement a near-certainty."
  },
  "reasoningStep": {

    "title": "Houston vs. Idaho — Round of 64 South Region Analysis",

The ~32-point efficiency gap, Houston's 4th-ranked defense nationally, and Idaho's 176th-ranked offense create one of the most extreme mismatches in the bracket with no credible upset path.",

      "Efficiency gap of ~32 points — one of the largest mismatches in the entire bracket",
      "Idaho won Big Sky Tournament as a 7-seed (beat Montana 77-66), first NCAA bid since 1990 — admirable but schedule never approached Big 12 caliber",
      "Houston reached Big 12 Tournament final, losing to #3 Arizona 79-74 — demonstrating elite-level competition readiness",
      "No significant Houston injury concerns identified; Jay Bilas and market consensus strongly favor Houston",
      "Historical 2-vs-15 record is overwhelmingly in favor of the higher seed; Idaho's offensive profile (176th nationally) vs. Houston's 4th-ranked defense offers no viable scoring path"
    ]
  }
}
```
